summary: Joel Spolsky reflects on the limits of hands-off management, noting that while it works well with trusted internal teams of smart, dedicated people, it fails when working with outside vendors. He and his co-founder Ryan discovered this during a trip, prompting them to reconsider when micromanagement is actually appropriate. The post serves as a teaser for his full Inc. magazine column on the topic.
key_points: Hands-off management works well with smart, dedicated internal staff but breaks down with external vendors
Even experienced entrepreneurs must continuously learn and adapt their management style to different contexts
Micromanagement, usually seen as negative, has legitimate use cases depending on who you're managing
money_quotes: We're both used to hiring very smart and dedicated people who will get things done to a high standard if you give them some general direction and set them free.
This style of hands-off management, which works so well with our own staffs, just wasn't working when we had outside vendors involved.
key_insight: The right management style depends on context: hands-off leadership that empowers internal teams can fail completely when applied to external vendors, making selective micromanagement a necessary skill.
